label: Diagnostic Foundations — Lesson Outlines
generationRule: All lessons teach RECOGNITION and DIFFERENTIAL REASONING only. Management is scoped to initial triage disposition (transfer vs test). No prescribing content.

lessons.title: Chest Pain Differential: Life-Threatening Causes
learningObjectives: Distinguish ACS from PE, aortic dissection, tension pneumothorax, and cardiac tamponade using history, physical, and targeted diagnostic findings
Apply the HEART score to risk-stratify chest pain in the outpatient setting
Identify clinical features that mandate immediate 911 activation vs urgent ED transfer
Recognize atypical ACS presentations in women, diabetics, and elderly patients
Select the appropriate initial diagnostic workup (ECG, troponin, D-dimer, CXR) based on pretest probability

keyPoints: The 'six killers': ACS, PE, aortic dissection, tension pneumothorax, esophageal rupture, cardiac tamponade
Feature-based exclusion order: start with ECG findings, then hemodynamic stability, then symptom character
Any diaphoresis + chest pain = ACS until proven otherwise
Classic: substernal pressure, radiation to jaw/left arm, diaphoresis, nausea, relieved by nitroglycerin
Atypical presentations: women (jaw pain, fatigue, dyspnea), elderly (dyspnea alone, syncope), diabetics (silent MI)
STEMI vs NSTEMI vs UA: ECG + troponin + symptom timeline
HEART score components: History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin — low (0–3), intermediate (4–6), high (7–10)
Classic: sudden-onset tearing/ripping pain, radiating to back, maximal at onset (vs crescendo in ACS)
Unequal blood pressures between arms (>20 mmHg difference) — 15% sensitive but highly specific
Pulse deficits, neurological deficits, new aortic regurgitation murmur
Risk factors: uncontrolled HTN, Marfan syndrome, bicuspid aortic valve, cocaine use
D-dimer has high sensitivity; definitive: CT aortogram — do not delay for treatment
Pleuritic chest pain + dyspnea + tachycardia; hemoptysis suggests pulmonary infarction
Wells score: DVT symptoms, PE most likely, HR>100, immobilization, prior DVT/PE, hemoptysis, cancer
Low Wells + low pretest → D-dimer (if negative, rules out); high Wells → CT pulmonary angiography
ECG: sinus tachycardia most common; S1Q3T3 in massive PE
Tension PTX: absent breath sounds unilaterally + tracheal deviation AWAY from affected side + hypotension + JVD — immediate needle decompression
Cardiac tamponade: Beck's triad (hypotension + muffled heart sounds + JVD); pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg; electrical alternans on ECG
Both require emergent disposition — not workup-and-wait

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: This lesson covers recognition and initial diagnostic workup only. Definitive management (thrombolytics, anticoagulation, cardiac catheterization) is covered in Phase 5 urgent care and Phase 3 chronic disease.

lessons.title: Chest Pain Differential: Non-Cardiac and Subacute Causes
learningObjectives: Distinguish musculoskeletal, gastrointestinal, pulmonary, and psychiatric causes of chest pain from cardiac causes
Use symptom characteristics (reproducibility, food relation, positional changes) to narrow the differential
Recognize pericarditis and myocarditis presentations in young patients
Identify when a 'benign-appearing' chest pain complaint requires further cardiac evaluation
Apply a systematic non-cardiac chest pain workup approach in the outpatient NP setting

keyPoints: GERD: burning, substernal, worse after meals/lying flat, relieved by antacids/PPI — can mimic ACS
Esophageal spasm: severe squeezing pain, can radiate to jaw, may respond to nitroglycerin (diagnostic trap)
Peptic ulcer: epigastric predominance, food relation varies (worse with food in gastric ulcer, better in duodenal)
Key differentiator: GERD/esophageal causes do NOT cause ECG changes or troponin elevation
Costochondritis: point tenderness at costochondral junction, reproducible with palpation — most common benign cause
Tietze syndrome: localized swelling at single costochondral junction, typically 2nd or 3rd rib
Rib fracture: hx of trauma or osteoporosis, point tenderness, pain with respiration
Herpes zoster: dermatomal distribution, burning quality, may precede rash by 2–3 days — do not miss
Pericarditis: sharp, pleuritic pain, worse supine, better leaning forward; pericardial friction rub; ECG: diffuse ST elevation (saddle-shaped) + PR depression
Pleuritis: sharp, positional, reproducible with deep inspiration; associated with infection, autoimmune disease, or PE
Myocarditis in young patients: viral prodrome + chest pain + elevated troponin with normal coronary angiogram
Panic disorder: palpitations, diaphoresis, shortness of breath, fear of dying — can be indistinguishable from ACS acutely
Key: cardiac workup must be completed before attributing to panic — do not anchor
Hyperventilation: paresthesias, carpopedal spasm, lightheadedness with chest tightness
Any new chest pain in patient >40 with ≥2 cardiac risk factors: ECG + troponin
Cocaine use: ACS at any age — vasospasm mechanism
Exertional component: requires stress testing regardless of other features
Atypical features in high-risk patients do not exclude ACS

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Questions in 1B must not duplicate 1A stems. 1B focuses on non-cardiac causes and the diagnostic reasoning around distinguishing them from cardiac causes.

lessons.title: Dyspnea Differential: Cardiac Causes
learningObjectives: Differentiate heart failure from arrhythmia, pericardial disease, and ACS as causes of acute dyspnea
Use BNP/NT-proBNP, chest X-ray, and ECG to narrow dyspnea differential toward cardiac etiology
Recognize HFrEF vs HFpEF clinical presentations
Identify patients with dyspnea who require emergent vs urgent evaluation
Distinguish cardiac from pulmonary causes using history and targeted physical exam findings

keyPoints: Rate of onset: acute (minutes → PE, flash pulmonary edema, pneumothorax), subacute (hours/days → HF exacerbation, pneumonia), chronic (weeks/months → COPD, HFpEF, anemia, malignancy)
Orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea: strongly suggest HF (specificity ~90%)
BNP <100 pg/mL: very unlikely HF; BNP >400 pg/mL: likely HF — intermediate zone requires clinical correlation
HFrEF (EF <40%): S3 gallop, lateral displacement of PMI, dilated cardiomegaly on CXR, low voltage ECG
HFpEF (EF ≥50%): diastolic dysfunction, preserved heart size, often in elderly women with HTN and DM
CXR findings: cardiomegaly, Kerley B lines, cephalization of pulmonary vessels, pleural effusions
Triggers for acute decompensation: dietary sodium excess, medication non-adherence, new arrhythmia, infection, ischemia

sections.sectionTitle: Arrhythmia as a Cause of Dyspnea
keyPoints: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response: irregular rhythm, palpitations, dyspnea — ECG shows irregularly irregular rate
Supraventricular tachycardia: sudden onset + sudden offset, regular rhythm, rates 150–250
High-degree AV block: bradycardia-induced dyspnea and presyncope
Pericardial effusion causing dyspnea: gradual accumulation until tamponade physiology develops
Beck's triad as late finding; pulsus paradoxus early finding
Echocardiogram: diagnostic of choice for pericardial effusion and tamponade assessment

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 1A focuses on cardiac causes of dyspnea. Pulmonary, metabolic, and other causes are in 1B. Questions in 1A must not use the same demographic + finding as chest pain 1A questions.

lessons.title: Dyspnea Differential: Pulmonary, Metabolic, and Other Causes
learningObjectives: Distinguish COPD exacerbation from asthma, pneumonia, and PE as acute causes of dyspnea
Recognize anemia, metabolic acidosis, obesity hypoventilation, and anxiety as non-cardiopulmonary dyspnea causes
Apply spirometry concepts and clinical history to differentiate obstructive from restrictive patterns
Identify the Wells score and D-dimer role in PE evaluation
Select appropriate initial workup for dyspnea based on acuity and clinical context

keyPoints: COPD exacerbation: increased dyspnea + increased sputum production + purulent sputum; history of smoking; pursed lip breathing, barrel chest, prolonged expiration
Asthma: episodic, reversible, triggers (allergen, cold air, exercise, aspirin); expiratory wheeze; peak flow monitoring
Pneumonia: productive cough, fever, crackles, consolidation on CXR; dyspnea proportional to consolidation extent
PE: sudden onset, pleuritic component, hemoptysis, risk factors (immobilization, prior DVT/PE, cancer, OCP use)
COPD: >40 years, smoking hx, irreversible obstruction (FEV1/FVC <0.7 post-bronchodilator)
Asthma: younger, atopic hx, fully reversible (FEV1 improvement ≥12% and 200 mL after bronchodilator)
ACO (asthma-COPD overlap): features of both; requires specialist input
Anemia: pallor, fatigue, exertional dyspnea proportional to Hgb level; tachycardia at rest if severe
Metabolic acidosis: compensatory hyperventilation (Kussmaul breathing); check pH, bicarbonate, anion gap
Obesity hypoventilation: BMI >40, daytime hypercapnia, polycythemia, cor pulmonale in chronic cases
Anxiety/hyperventilation: paresthesias, carpopedal spasm, no hypoxia — diagnosis of exclusion after workup
Immediate: pulse oximetry, respiratory rate, accessory muscle use
First-line: ECG, CXR, CBC, BMP, BNP
Targeted by presentation: D-dimer + CT-PA if PE suspected; PFTs if chronic obstructive pattern; echo if cardiac cause suspected
Disposition signal: O2 sat <90%, accessory muscle use, altered mental status → emergency transfer

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 1B must not reuse PE questions from chest pain 1A. Each dyspnea question must use a distinct chief complaint anchor not used in chest pain topics.

lessons.title: Abdominal Pain Differential: Adult and Surgical Emergencies
learningObjectives: Recognize the presentation of appendicitis, bowel obstruction, mesenteric ischemia, perforated viscus, and ruptured AAA
Apply anatomical location to generate an age-appropriate differential for abdominal pain
Identify peritoneal signs (guarding, rigidity, rebound) and their clinical significance
Distinguish biliary from GI from vascular causes of acute abdominal pain
Determine which presentations require immediate transfer vs in-office workup

keyPoints: RUQ: cholecystitis, cholangitis, hepatitis, Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome, right lower lobe pneumonia (referred)
RLQ: appendicitis, Crohn's, ovarian pathology (females), Meckel's diverticulum (pediatric), psoas abscess
LLQ: diverticulitis, sigmoid volvulus, ovarian pathology (females), ischemic colitis
LUQ: splenic pathology (infarct, rupture), pancreatitis (left-sided), gastric pathology
Epigastric: peptic ulcer, pancreatitis, GERD, ACS (referred), early appendicitis
Diffuse/periumbilical: early appendicitis, mesenteric ischemia, bowel obstruction, ruptured AAA
Classic: periumbilical pain → RLQ migration + anorexia + nausea, low-grade fever
Rovsing's sign, psoas sign, obturator sign — clinical aids
Alvarado score: WBC + shift + rebound + migration + anorexia + nausea = 0–10
Perforation risk increases after 24–36 hours; elderly and young children have atypical presentations and higher perforation rates
Ruptured AAA: sudden severe back/abdominal pain + pulsatile abdominal mass + hemodynamic instability — 50% mortality even with surgery
Mesenteric ischemia: pain out of proportion to exam, risk factors (AF, hypercoagulable, atherosclerosis), lactic acidosis late finding
Perforated viscus: sudden onset severe diffuse pain, rigid board-like abdomen, free air on upright CXR under diaphragm
Bowel obstruction: colicky pain + distension + vomiting + obstipation; high-pitched bowel sounds early, absent late
Cholecystitis: RUQ pain, Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation), fever, elevated WBC
Choledocholithiasis: RUQ pain + jaundice (no fever typically)
Ascending cholangitis (Charcot's triad): RUQ pain + fever + jaundice; Reynold's pentad adds hypotension + altered mental status — septic emergency

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 1A focuses on adult abdominal pain and surgical emergencies. Gynecological and pediatric causes are in 1B. No overlap in question demographics or chief complaints between 1A and 1B.

lessons.title: Abdominal Pain Differential: Gynecological and Pediatric Presentations
learningObjectives: Recognize ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion, and PID as gynecological emergencies presenting with abdominal pain
Distinguish age-specific pediatric causes: intussusception, volvulus, Hirschsprung disease, Meckel's diverticulum
Apply the pregnancy test as the critical first step in all women of reproductive age with abdominal pain
Identify clinical features that distinguish functional from surgical abdominal pain in pediatric patients
Select appropriate workup and disposition for gynecological and pediatric abdominal emergencies

keyPoints: Ectopic pregnancy: amenorrhea + lower abdominal pain + vaginal spotting; rupture → hemodynamic instability and referred shoulder pain (diaphragmatic irritation from hemoperitoneum)
β-hCG positive + no intrauterine pregnancy on US = ectopic until proven otherwise
Ovarian torsion: sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, may radiate to back/thigh; nausea/vomiting common; normal WBC; US shows enlarged ovary with absent or reduced Doppler flow
PID: cervical motion tenderness (CMT), adnexal tenderness, uterine tenderness — Chandelier sign; usually bilateral; sexually active women
Tubo-ovarian abscess: PID + mass + systemic illness + failure to improve on antibiotics → surgery or IR drainage
Newborn/infant: Hirschsprung disease (failure to pass meconium >48h, abdominal distension), malrotation/volvulus (bilious vomiting = emergency until proven otherwise)
Infant (2–36 months): intussusception — episodic severe colicky crying, knees to chest, vomiting, currant jelly stool (late), sausage-shaped mass RUQ
School age: appendicitis (most common surgical emergency), Meckel's diverticulum (painless rectal bleeding), mesenteric lymphadenitis (viral illness + LAD + periumbilical pain)
Adolescent female: same as adult gynecological causes; always test β-hCG
Functional abdominal pain: most common cause of recurrent abdominal pain in school-age children; periumbilical, no weight loss, no nocturnal awakening, normal exam and labs
Rome IV criteria: recurrent without structural/metabolic cause
Red flags warranting workup: RLQ localization, weight loss, fever, blood in stool, family hx of IBD, nocturnal pain

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: All gynecological question stems must specify reproductive-age female. All pediatric stems must specify age clearly to prevent overlap with adult 1A questions.

lessons.title: Headache Differential: Primary Headache Syndromes
learningObjectives: Apply the SNOOP4 mnemonic to identify secondary headache red flags
Distinguish migraine from tension-type headache from cluster headache using clinical criteria
Recognize the impact of hormonal factors, sleep, and dietary triggers on primary headache
Identify medication overuse headache (MOH) as a secondary headache pattern requiring recognition
Determine when a primary headache diagnosis can be made clinically vs when imaging is required

keyPoints: S: Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) or Systemic disease (HIV, cancer)
N: Neurological symptoms or signs (focal deficit, altered consciousness)
O: Onset sudden or 'thunderclap'
O: Older age new headache (>50 years — consider GCA, mass)
P: Progressive worsening pattern or positional component
P: Papilledema
P: Precipitation by Valsalva/exertion/positional change
P: Postural component
POUND mnemonic: Pulsating, duration 4–72 hOUrs, Unilateral, Nausea/vomiting, Disabling
Migraine with aura: visual (fortification spectra, scotoma), sensory, or language symptoms lasting 20–60 min preceding headache
Triggers: hormonal fluctuation (perimenstrual), sleep disruption, fasting, bright light, red wine, tyramine-containing foods
OCP and migraine with aura: absolute contraindication to combined oral contraceptives (stroke risk)
Bilateral, pressure/band-like quality, mild–moderate intensity, no nausea or vomiting, not aggravated by routine activity
No aura, no photophobia AND phonophobia (one may be present but not both)
Episodic (<15 days/month) vs chronic (≥15 days/month)
Most common headache type overall; diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out secondary causes
Severe unilateral periorbital/temporal pain; autonomic features IPSILATERAL: lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, miosis, conjunctival injection
Duration 15–180 minutes; frequency 1–8x/day; occur at same time daily ('alarm clock headache')
Circadian clustering: most common in spring/fall; alcohol triggers during a cluster period
More common in men aged 20–40; restless/agitated behavior distinguishes from migraine (migraine patients are still)
Use of analgesics or triptans ≥10–15 days/month for >3 months → paradoxical daily headache
Often in patients with episodic migraine or TTH who escalate analgesic use
Diagnosis: new daily persistent headache in known headache patient using frequent analgesics
Recognition point: the treatment itself has become the cause

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 1A covers primary headache syndromes. Red flag/secondary headache causes are in 1B. No question in 1A should involve thunderclap, fever, papilledema, or neurological deficits.

lessons.title: Headache Differential: Secondary and Dangerous Causes
learningObjectives: Recognize subarachnoid hemorrhage as the most dangerous headache emergency: thunderclap pattern and LP findings
Identify giant cell arteritis in patients >50 using jaw claudication, temporal artery tenderness, and ESR
Distinguish bacterial meningitis headache from other causes using Kernig, Brudzinski, and nuchal rigidity
Recognize increased intracranial pressure headache pattern and papilledema significance
Apply neuroimaging indications for new or changing headache

keyPoints: 'Worst headache of my life' — thunderclap onset, maximal severity at onset (seconds)
Sentinel bleed may present as less severe headache days before rupture — do not miss
Workup: non-contrast CT head first; if negative within 6 hours of onset, 95% sensitive; if >6 hours → LP for xanthochromia
LP: xanthochromia (yellow CSF from oxyhemoglobin) + elevated RBCs that do NOT clear between tubes 1 and 4
Any thunderclap headache: do NOT give triptans or analgesics before ruling out SAH
Classic triad: headache + fever + neck stiffness (only 44% have all three; 95% have at least 2)
Kernig's sign: knee cannot be extended with hip at 90° (meningeal irritation)
Brudzinski's sign: passive neck flexion causes involuntary knee flexion
Petechial/purpuric rash: meningococcal meningitis — emergent; do not delay antibiotics for LP
Photophobia and phonophobia also present — differs from migraine by fever and meningeal signs
Age >50 years (almost never younger), women > men
New temporal headache + jaw claudication + scalp tenderness + visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax, diplopia)
Temporal artery: tender, nodular, non-pulsatile on palpation
ESR >50 mm/hr (often >100); CRP elevated; definitive: temporal artery biopsy
Do NOT wait for biopsy to start high-dose prednisone — visual loss is irreversible
Headache worse in morning, lying down, or with Valsalva; projectile vomiting without nausea
Papilledema: blurred disc margins on fundoscopy — urgent ophthalmology referral
Causes: space-occupying lesion (mass, abscess, hematoma), idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri — obese women of childbearing age)
Cushing's reflex (late): HTN + bradycardia + irregular breathing = impending herniation

sections.sectionTitle: Hypertensive Headache and Other Secondary Causes
keyPoints: Hypertensive encephalopathy: BP usually >180/120 + headache + visual changes + altered mental status
Carbon monoxide: headache in multiple household members simultaneously; diffuse bilateral, frontal
Drug-induced headache: OCP (migraines worsen), vasodilators (nitroglycerin), NSAIDs (MOH if overused)
Neuroimaging indications: thunderclap, focal deficit, papilledema, immunocompromised, age >50 new headache, cancer history

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: 1B is exclusively secondary/dangerous headaches. Each question must have a red flag feature in the stem. No question may overlap demographically with 1A primary headache questions.

lessons.title: Fatigue Differential: Systematic Approach in Primary Care
learningObjectives: Generate a systematic differential for fatigue covering endocrine, hematological, psychiatric, and infectious causes
Use targeted history findings and screening labs to distinguish thyroid disease, anemia, diabetes, and depression as causes of fatigue
Recognize red flag fatigue features suggesting malignancy or serious systemic disease
Apply an efficient initial lab panel for undifferentiated fatigue in primary care
Identify when fatigue represents a symptom of an emerging condition requiring urgent workup

sections.sectionTitle: Initial Lab Panel for Undifferentiated Fatigue
keyPoints: CBC (anemia, leukemia), CMP (renal, hepatic, glucose), TSH (thyroid), fasting glucose or HbA1c (diabetes), ferritin (IDA), B12/folate
PHQ-9 (depression screen) — should be done simultaneously with lab workup, not after labs are 'normal'
Additional based on history: EBV/mono spot, HIV, hepatitis panel, ESR/CRP (inflammatory/autoimmune)
Hypothyroidism: fatigue + cold intolerance + constipation + weight gain + dry skin + hair loss + bradycardia + myxedema; TSH elevated, free T4 low
Hyperthyroidism: fatigue + heat intolerance + weight loss + tremor + palpitations + diarrhea; TSH suppressed, free T4/T3 elevated
Subclinical hypothyroidism: elevated TSH, normal free T4, nonspecific symptoms — treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic

sections.sectionTitle: Anemia as a Cause of Fatigue
keyPoints: IDA: microcytic (MCV <80), low ferritin (<12 ng/mL), pica, pagophagia, heavy menstrual bleeding in women, GI blood loss in men/older adults
B12/folate deficiency: macrocytic (MCV >100), neurological symptoms (subacute combined degeneration — B12), glossitis, vegetarian/vegan diet
Anemia of chronic disease: normocytic, low iron, low TIBC, elevated ferritin (opposite of IDA)
Hemolytic anemia: jaundice, splenomegaly, elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin
Uncontrolled T2DM: fatigue + polyuria + polydipsia + blurred vision; HbA1c elevated
Hypothalamic-pituitary: hypopituitarism causes fatigue with other hormonal deficiencies (hypogonadism, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism together)
CKD-related fatigue: correlates with eGFR decline; often overlooked — check creatinine in any fatigue workup
Obstructive sleep apnea: fatigue + excessive daytime sleepiness + witnessed apneas + loud snoring; BMI is a risk factor but not required
MDD: fatigue + depressed mood or anhedonia; PHQ-9 ≥10; rule out medical causes first but do not delay PHQ-9 screening
EBV/infectious mononucleosis: fatigue + pharyngitis + cervical posterior lymphadenopathy + splenomegaly; atypical lymphocytes on peripheral smear; heterophile antibody test
Red flag fatigue features: unintentional weight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy, hemoptysis → malignancy workup

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Fatigue questions must specify both the fatigue quality AND the associated symptom cluster that narrows the differential. No question may use the same associated symptom combination as another Phase 1 topic (e.g., fever + fatigue is covered in Fever Without Source topic).

lessons.title: Fever Without Source: Viral vs Bacterial vs Systemic Cause
learningObjectives: Apply age-stratified risk assessment for fever without an identifiable source
Distinguish viral from bacterial causes using clinical features, WBC patterns, and procalcitonin
Recognize fever patterns suggesting malignancy, autoimmune disease, or travel-related illness
Identify high-risk fever presentations requiring immediate workup and empiric treatment
Determine appropriate workup and disposition for fever without source by clinical context

keyPoints: Definition: fever (≥38°C/100.4°F) without a clear focus after history, physical, and initial assessment
Acute (<7 days): usually infectious; subacute/prolonged (>3 weeks): consider FUO workup (infection, malignancy, autoimmune, drug fever)
FUO diagnostic criteria: ≥38.3°C on multiple occasions, duration >3 weeks, no diagnosis after 3 outpatient visits or 3 days hospitalization
Viral: gradual onset, self-limiting, WBC normal or mildly elevated with lymphocyte predominance, procalcitonin <0.25 ng/mL
Bacterial: higher fever, chills/rigors, WBC >12,000 with left shift, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, localized source usually identifiable
Procalcitonin: rises rapidly (4–6 hrs) in bacterial infection; useful to guide antibiotic stewardship; not useful for localized infections (UTI, skin)
Infant <3 months with fever ≥38°C: serious bacterial infection (SBI) risk — requires full septic workup (CBC, CMP, UA/culture, blood culture, consider LP)
Febrile neutropenia: ANC <500 + fever = oncologic emergency; empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics
Returned traveler with fever: malaria until proven otherwise (can be fatal if delayed); also dengue, typhoid, rickettsial disease
IVDU with fever: endocarditis until proven otherwise; blood cultures ×2 before antibiotics; echocardiogram
Lymphoma (B symptoms): fever + drenching night sweats + ≥10% weight loss + lymphadenopathy
SLE flare: fever + malar rash + joint pain + serositis; check ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement levels
Drug fever: temporal relation to medication start; common culprits: beta-lactams, phenytoin, sulfonamides, allopurinol; eosinophilia may be present
Toxic shock syndrome: fever + hypotension + diffuse erythematous rash + multiorgan dysfunction; tampon use or skin wound as source

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Fever questions must include a clinical context detail (age, travel history, occupation, immunosuppression, IV drug use) that anchors the differential. No fever stem may use the same context as a stem in any other Phase 1 topic.

lessons.title: Syncope Differential: Vasovagal, Cardiac, and Neurological
learningObjectives: Classify syncope by mechanism: reflex (vasovagal), orthostatic, cardiac arrhythmic, cardiac structural, and neurological
Apply the San Francisco Syncope Rule to identify high-risk features requiring ED evaluation
Distinguish true syncope from seizure, hypoglycemic loss of consciousness, and TIA mimics
Use ECG findings to identify arrhythmic causes of syncope
Determine which syncope presentations can be evaluated outpatient vs require emergent evaluation

keyPoints: True syncope: transient loss of consciousness due to global cerebral hypoperfusion; self-limited; recovery within seconds
Reflex/vasovagal: prodrome (nausea, diaphoresis, warmth, yawning); trigger (prolonged standing, heat, emotional stress, venipuncture); most common type
Orthostatic: sustained drop in BP ≥20 mmHg systolic or ≥10 mmHg diastolic within 3 minutes of standing; causes (dehydration, medications, autonomic neuropathy)
Cardiac arrhythmic: no prodrome, exertional, older patients; family hx sudden cardiac death; ECG abnormalities
Cardiac structural: exertional syncope + murmur = aortic stenosis or HOCM until proven otherwise
C: CHF history
H: Hematocrit <30%
E: Abnormal ECG
S: Shortness of breath
S: Systolic BP <90 at triage
Any positive: high risk → ED evaluation
Seizure: tonic-clonic movements before LOC (not after), post-ictal confusion (prolonged), tongue biting, urinary incontinence, headache after
Convulsive syncope: brief tonic-clonic-like movements AFTER LOC due to cerebral hypoperfusion — EEG normal, no post-ictal confusion
TIA: focal neurological deficits during event (slurred speech, arm weakness, visual loss) — NOT a cause of syncope (TIA causes focal deficits, not global cerebral hypoperfusion)
Prolonged QT (>470 ms women, >450 ms men): risk of torsades de pointes
Brugada pattern: RBBB morphology + ST elevation in V1–V2 (coved type)
Complete heart block or high-degree AV block: Stokes-Adams attacks
Pre-excitation (WPW delta wave): risk of SVT degenerating to VF
New LBBB: may indicate structural heart disease or ischemia

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Syncope questions must specify whether prodrome was present (vasovagal) or absent (cardiac). Seizure-mimicking syncope questions must distinguish by post-event behavior. No syncope question may reuse a demographic already used in dyspnea or chest pain questions.

lessons.title: Flank Pain Differential: UTI, Nephrolithiasis, Pyelonephritis, and Beyond
learningObjectives: Distinguish nephrolithiasis from pyelonephritis, uncomplicated UTI, and renal pathology using history and targeted exam
Identify clinical features suggesting malignant, vascular, or musculoskeletal causes of flank pain
Apply urine dipstick, urinalysis, and imaging selection criteria for flank pain evaluation
Recognize when flank pain requires immediate transfer (suspected ruptured AAA, urosepsis)
Determine appropriate outpatient management vs ED disposition for nephrolithiasis

keyPoints: Nephrolithiasis: sudden severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin/ipsilateral testicle or labia; patient restless/unable to find comfortable position; hematuria (80%); nausea/vomiting; NO fever unless infection superimposed
Pyelonephritis: fever + rigors + flank pain + CVA tenderness + urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency); less colicky, more constant
Uncomplicated UTI: lower tract symptoms only (dysuria, frequency, urgency), no fever >38°C, no CVA tenderness
Key differentiator: fever + flank pain = pyelonephritis; afebrile + colicky + radiation to groin = stone
Urinalysis: hematuria (absent in 20% of stones — don't rule out), possible pH clue to stone type
Imaging: low-dose non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis (gold standard); renal US first-line for pregnant women and radiation concerns
Stone size management: <5 mm → 90% pass spontaneously; 5–10 mm → 50% pass; >10 mm → urological intervention typically needed
Indication for ED transfer from office: suspected urosepsis (stone + fever), uncontrolled pain, solitary kidney, bilateral obstruction, anuric
Renal cell carcinoma: painless hematuria + flank pain + palpable mass (classic triad — often late presentation); weight loss, fatigue, night sweats
ADPKD: bilateral flank pain + bilateral palpable kidneys + family history + HTN + hematuria
Ruptured AAA masquerading as flank pain: sudden severe pain radiating to flank + hemodynamic instability + pulsatile mass — emergent surgery
Musculoskeletal: reproducible with position change, paraspinal muscle tenderness, hx trauma or heavy lifting, no urinary symptoms

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Flank pain questions must specify fever status and urinary symptom presence to anchor the differential. No flank pain stem may duplicate the UTI or pyelonephritis stems used in Acute Primary Care Phase 5 topics.

lessons.title: Knee Pain Differential: Structural, Inflammatory, and Infectious Causes
learningObjectives: Distinguish traumatic structural causes (ACL, meniscus, patellar fracture) from inflammatory arthritis and septic arthritis
Apply the Ottawa Knee Rules to select patients who require radiographic evaluation
Recognize septic arthritis as a surgical emergency requiring immediate joint aspiration
Differentiate monoarthritis from polyarthritis presentations and their differential implications
Identify referred pain from hip and lumbar spine as a cause of knee pain

keyPoints: ACL tear: twisting mechanism, immediate swelling (hemarthrosis), positive Lachman test (most sensitive), anterior drawer sign, pivot shift
Meniscus tear: joint line tenderness, McMurray test (pain/click with rotation), locking or giving way; effusion develops over hours vs immediate in ACL
PCL tear: posterior sag on exam, mechanism often dashboard injury or forced hyperextension
Ottawa Knee Rules: X-ray needed if: age ≥55, isolated patellar tenderness, fibular head tenderness, cannot flex to 90°, or cannot weight-bear 4 steps
Osteoarthritis: older patient, mechanical pain (worse with activity, better with rest), morning stiffness <30 minutes, crepitus, bony enlargement, Heberden/Bouchard nodes
Rheumatoid arthritis: symmetric, small joints primarily but can involve knees; morning stiffness >1 hour, RF/anti-CCP antibodies
Gout: sudden onset monoarthritis, first MTP classic but any joint; joint aspiration: negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals (MSU)
Pseudogout: calcium pyrophosphate crystals, positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped; often knee; radiograph: chondrocalcinosis
Single hot swollen joint + fever = septic until proven otherwise
Gonococcal arthritis in young sexually active adults: migratory arthritis preceding monoarthritis + skin lesions (pustules) + tenosynovitis
Non-gonococcal: S. aureus most common; IVDU: gram-negative organisms possible
Joint aspiration: WBC >50,000/mm³ suggests septic; send culture, glucose, protein, crystal analysis
Do NOT delay aspiration — cartilage destruction occurs within hours of untreated septic arthritis
Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS): anterior knee pain worse with stairs, squatting, prolonged sitting ('movie sign'); young athletes and women
Osgood-Schlatter: tibial tuberosity swelling and tenderness in adolescent athletes during growth spurt
Baker's cyst: posterior knee fullness; rupture mimics DVT (calf swelling, edema) — ultrasound differentiates
Referred pain: hip OA (anterior knee pain, normal knee exam, limited internal hip rotation); lumbar radiculopathy (dermatomal pattern)

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: All knee pain questions must specify age, mechanism (traumatic vs atraumatic), and joint involvement pattern (mono vs poly). Septic arthritis questions must include fever. No knee pain stem may duplicate the gout or OA stems in Phase 3 chronic disease topics.

lessons.title: Rash Differential: Morphology-Based Primary Care Approach
learningObjectives: Apply a morphology-based framework (macule, papule, vesicle, pustule, purpura) to narrow rash differential
Identify immediately life-threatening rashes requiring emergency transfer (meningococcemia, SJS/TEN, NF)
Distinguish viral from bacterial from inflammatory rashes using distribution, associated symptoms, and appearance
Recognize contact dermatitis, atopic dermatitis, and psoriasis as common chronic rash presentations
Determine which rash presentations require dermatology referral vs primary care management

keyPoints: Macule/patch: flat color change — viral exanthems, hyperpigmentation, tinea versicolor
Papule/plaque: raised palpable — psoriasis, lichen planus, eczema, seborrheic dermatitis
Vesicle/bulla: fluid-filled — herpes simplex/zoster, bullous pemphigoid, contact dermatitis
Pustule: purulent fluid — folliculitis, impetigo, acne, disseminated gonococcemia
Purpura/petechiae: non-blanching — ITP, vasculitis, meningococcemia, RMSF (requires immediate action)
Target lesion: erythema multiforme (minor) vs SJS (extensive mucosal involvement, >10% BSA skin detachment)
Meningococcemia: fever + non-blanching purpuric rash ± meningismus — call 911, empiric antibiotics before transfer
SJS/TEN: medication reaction (sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs); mucosal involvement, skin detachment, Nikolsky sign positive
Necrotizing fasciitis: severe pain out of proportion to exam, skin changes (bronze → bullae → necrosis), systemic toxicity, crepitus — emergent surgery
RMSF: tick bite + fever + rash starting on wrists/ankles spreading centrally — thrombocytopenia, hyponatremia
Herpes zoster: dermatomal vesicular rash, burning/pruritic, allodynia preceding rash by 1–3 days
Primary syphilis: painless indurated chancre + bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy; may be missed
Secondary syphilis: diffuse maculopapular rash involving palms and soles — pathognomonic pattern
Lyme disease: erythema migrans (expanding annular target lesion ≥5 cm, central clearing); tick exposure
Scarlet fever: sandpaper-like rash + strawberry tongue + pharyngitis; Group A Strep
Psoriasis: well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale; Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale removed); extensor surfaces, scalp, nails (onycholysis)
Atopic dermatitis: flexor surfaces, intensely pruritic, lichenification from chronic scratching; atopic triad with asthma and allergic rhinitis
Contact dermatitis: linear or geometric pattern matching allergen exposure (poison ivy), pruritic, vesicular

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Rash questions must specify the morphology type, distribution, and whether blanching is present. Life-threatening rash questions (meningococcemia, SJS) must test recognition and disposition — not treatment.

lessons.title: Red Eye Differential: Primary Care Triage and Diagnosis
learningObjectives: Distinguish conjunctivitis (bacterial, viral, allergic) from uveitis, acute angle-closure glaucoma, and corneal pathology
Identify clinical features requiring immediate ophthalmology referral
Apply visual acuity assessment and fluorescein staining concepts to red eye evaluation
Recognize gonococcal conjunctivitis as a vision-threatening STI requiring urgent treatment
Determine which red eye presentations can be managed in primary care vs require referral

keyPoints: Vision-threatening red eye features: pain (not just discomfort), vision loss, photophobia, fixed or irregular pupil, ciliary flush — any of these requires same-day ophthalmology referral
Non-vision-threatening: discharge predominant, normal vision, bilateral, itching without pain, clear cornea
Bacterial: mucopurulent discharge (yellow/green), crusted lashes, unilateral or bilateral, morning crusting; common pathogens: S. aureus, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae (children)
Viral (adenoviral): watery/serous discharge, preauricular lymphadenopathy, often follows URI, highly contagious, bilateral, follicular response on palpation of palpebral conjunctiva
Allergic: bilateral, intense itching (key differentiator), stringy/watery discharge, papillary response, seasonal pattern or allergen exposure
Gonococcal: copious purulent discharge, rapid progression, chemosis; STI co-testing essential; vision-threatening (corneal perforation)
Anterior uveitis/iritis: unilateral, deep aching pain, photophobia, CILIARY FLUSH (perilimbal injection), irregular/constricted pupil, cells and flare on slit-lamp
Associated conditions: HLA-B27 (ankylosing spondylitis, reactive arthritis, IBD, psoriasis), sarcoidosis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis
Urgent ophthalmology referral — synechiae formation and vision loss if untreated
Sudden severe unilateral eye pain + headache + nausea/vomiting + halos around lights + blurred vision
Fixed mid-dilated pupil (5–6 mm); steamy/hazy cornea (corneal edema from elevated IOP)
IOP typically >40 mmHg (normal <21); triggered by dim lighting, mydriatics, medications
Ophthalmic emergency: permanent vision loss within hours — immediate referral while starting topical IOP-lowering agents
Corneal abrasion: foreign body sensation, photophobia, pain, fluorescein staining shows defect under Wood's lamp/cobalt blue light
Corneal ulcer: contact lens wearer + severe pain + purulent discharge + white corneal opacity — culture before antibiotics; urgent ophthalmology
Subconjunctival hemorrhage: bright red blood under conjunctiva; flat, unilateral; no pain; no vision change; benign if atraumatic; resolve in 1–3 weeks

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: All red eye questions must specify: unilateral or bilateral, presence or absence of pain (distinct from discomfort), and visual acuity status. Painless red eye = likely conjunctivitis or subconjunctival hemorrhage. Painful = iritis, glaucoma, corneal pathology — must test referral decision.

lessons.title: Hemoptysis Differential: Etiology, Risk Stratification, and Workup
learningObjectives: Generate a comprehensive differential for hemoptysis from airway, parenchymal, vascular, and cardiac causes
Distinguish true hemoptysis from hematemesis and nasopharyngeal bleeding
Apply risk stratification to determine outpatient vs emergency evaluation based on volume and clinical context
Identify red flag features suggesting malignancy, TB, or vasculitis requiring urgent workup
Select appropriate initial imaging and workup based on clinical probability

keyPoints: Hemoptysis: bright red, frothy, alkaline pH, preceded by coughing or gurgling sensation in chest
Hematemesis: dark red or coffee-ground, acidic pH, preceded by nausea, mixed with food particles
Epistaxis: nasal source — examine nasopharynx; posterior epistaxis can be swallowed then vomited or coughed
Massive hemoptysis: >200–600 mL/24h (definitions vary); immediate airway threat — emergent
Outpatient, most common: bronchitis (most common cause in primary care), pneumonia, bronchiectasis
High-risk patient (smoker >40): lung cancer until proven otherwise
Young immigrant/endemic area: TB (upper lobe cavitary lesion + night sweats + weight loss)
Recurrent hemoptysis + chronic productive cough + clubbing: bronchiectasis (dilated airways — CT diagnostic)
Hemoptysis after DVT/PE: pulmonary infarction (pleuritic pain + peripheral wedge-shaped opacity)
Mitral stenosis: pulmonary venous HTN causes hemoptysis; exertional dyspnea, opening snap, low-pitched diastolic murmur at apex
Goodpasture syndrome: hemoptysis + hematuria + anti-GBM antibodies; rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis
GPA (Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis/Wegener): hemoptysis + saddle nose deformity + sinusitis + renal disease; c-ANCA positive
HHT (Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia): telangiectasias on lips/tongue/hands; pulmonary AVMs; family history
All hemoptysis: CXR first — identifies mass, infiltrate, cavitation, cardiomegaly
CXR normal + smoker >40 or risk factors: CT chest with contrast + bronchoscopy
Suspected TB: sputum AFB smear × 3, cultures, respiratory isolation
Urgent labs: CBC (thrombocytopenia), PT/INR (coagulopathy), BUN/Cr (Goodpasture/vasculitis), ANCA if systemic features
Indications for immediate hospital transfer: hemoptysis >5 mL per episode, hemodynamic instability, O2 saturation decline, bilateral infiltrates

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Hemoptysis questions must specify volume (small streaking vs significant), patient age/smoking history, and any systemic symptoms. Malignancy and TB questions must have red flag features embedded in the stem to be appropriately expert-level.

lessons.title: Lymphadenopathy: Reactive vs Malignant Workup Algorithm
learningObjectives: Apply anatomical location of lymphadenopathy to generate an appropriate differential
Distinguish reactive from malignant lymphadenopathy using node characteristics, associated symptoms, and duration
Recognize B symptoms as a lymphoma red flag requiring urgent workup
Identify Virchow's node, Sister Mary Joseph nodule, and scalene node significance
Apply the initial workup algorithm and determine criteria for lymph node biopsy referral

keyPoints: Cervical (anterior): upper respiratory infections, EBV, oral/dental infections, thyroid pathology
Cervical (posterior): EBV (classic posterior cervical), rubella, toxoplasmosis, scalp infections
Supraclavicular: HIGH concern for malignancy — right: lung/esophageal; left (Virchow's node): gastric/abdominal/pelvic malignancy
Axillary: breast pathology, arm/chest wall infection, lymphoma, cat scratch disease
Inguinal: lower extremity/perineal infection, STIs (syphilis, LGV, genital herpes), lower extremity lymphoma
Mediastinal/hilar (on imaging): sarcoidosis, lymphoma, TB, histoplasmosis
Reactive (benign): soft, tender, mobile, <1 cm, associated recent infection, resolves in 4–6 weeks
Malignant (concerning): firm to rubbery (lymphoma) or rock-hard (metastatic), non-tender, fixed, >1 cm, no associated infection, persists >4–6 weeks or progressive
Supraclavicular nodes: always concerning regardless of size or tenderness
B symptoms: fever >38°C + drenching night sweats + ≥10% weight loss in 6 months → lymphoma workup
EBV (infectious mononucleosis): posterior cervical LAD + splenomegaly + pharyngitis + atypical lymphocytes + heterophile antibody
CMV: similar to EBV but heterophile negative; CMV-specific IgM diagnostic
Toxoplasmosis: cat exposure or undercooked meat; posterior cervical LAD; IgG/IgM serology
Cat scratch disease (Bartonella henselae): cat scratch/bite → regional LAD + fever + papule at inoculation site; treatment: azithromycin for severe cases
HIV: acute HIV with seroconversion syndrome — generalized LAD + fever + pharyngitis + rash
Initial workup: CBC with differential (atypical lymphocytes, blast cells), LDH and uric acid (lymphoma), ESR/CRP, monospot, EBV/CMV serology, CXR
Biopsy indications: persistence >4–6 weeks without explanation, size >1–2 cm without infection, supraclavicular location, B symptoms, associated constitutional symptoms, rock-hard consistency
Excisional biopsy preferred over FNA for lymph node evaluation when lymphoma is suspected (need architecture)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: required for staging evaluation once malignancy is suspected

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: Lymphadenopathy questions must specify node location, size, consistency (tender/non-tender, mobile/fixed), duration, and associated symptoms. The high-yield teaching distinction is reactive vs malignant, so each question should test one arm of that distinction clearly.

lessons.title: Endocrine Crisis Differentials: Recognition and Triage in Primary Care
learningObjectives: Distinguish adrenal insufficiency from thyroid storm, myxedema coma, DKA, and HHS using presenting features
Recognize hypoglycemic crisis as a common endocrine emergency with specific recognition features
Identify the clinical findings that differentiate primary vs secondary adrenal insufficiency
Apply recognition-level decision-making to determine disposition for endocrine emergencies encountered in primary care
Recognize pheochromocytoma crisis as a treatable cause of hypertensive emergency

keyPoints: Primary AI (Addison's disease): fatigue + hyperpigmentation (ACTH stimulates MSH — buccal mucosa, skin creases, recent scars) + hypotension + hyponatremia + HYPERKALEMIA
Secondary AI: fatigue + hypotension WITHOUT hyperpigmentation (ACTH deficiency, so no MSH stimulation) + hyponatremia but NOT hyperkalemia (aldosterone intact)
Adrenal crisis triggers: sudden steroid withdrawal, illness, surgery, trauma in a patient on chronic steroids
Key labs: low cortisol + high ACTH (primary) vs low cortisol + low ACTH (secondary); hyponatremia + hyperkalemia combination is classic for primary AI
Thyroid storm: fever >38.5°C + tachycardia/arrhythmia + HTN + altered mental status + GI symptoms (vomiting, diarrhea) in hyperthyroid patient; precipitated by infection, surgery, or iodine load
Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale: used to quantify likelihood of thyroid storm (temperature, heart rate, CNS effects, GI-hepatic dysfunction)
Myxedema coma: severe hypothyroidism + altered mental status + hypothermia + bradycardia + hypoventilation + hyponatremia; precipitated by cold exposure, infection, medications (sedatives)
Recognition distinction: thyroid storm = hyperadrenergic state; myxedema coma = hypometabolic shutdown
DKA: T1DM primarily (can occur in T2DM); glucose often 250–600; ANION GAP metabolic ACIDOSIS (pH <7.3, bicarbonate <18); Kussmaul breathing; fruity breath; ketonemia/ketonuria
HHS: T2DM exclusively; glucose often >600 (can be >1000); NO or minimal ketoacidosis; severe dehydration; altered mental status proportional to hyperosmolarity
Key distinction: acidosis present in DKA, absent in HHS; ketones positive in DKA, absent/minimal in HHS
Both require ED transfer — recognition in primary care initiates transfer while providing initial IV access if available
Severe hypoglycemia (<54 mg/dL with symptoms; <40 mg/dL with altered mental status): diaphoresis + tremor + tachycardia (adrenergic) → confusion → seizure → coma
In-office recognition: glucose meter; if responsive → oral glucose; if altered → glucagon 1 mg IM (if available); call 911
Pheochromocytoma crisis: paroxysmal hypertension + palpitations + headache + diaphoresis (classic triad); can be precipitated by stress, palpation, or certain medications (beta-blockers)
Recognition: episodic nature distinguishes from essential hypertension; screen with plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urine catecholamines

lessons.stemDistinctionNote: SCOPE FENCE: This lesson covers RECOGNITION and TRIAGE DISPOSITION ONLY. No question may test specific dose of hydrocortisone, propylthiouracil dosing, DKA fluid resuscitation rates, or any treatment protocol. Permitted question types: 'most likely diagnosis', 'which finding confirms the diagnosis', 'most appropriate immediate action' (answer must be 'transfer to ED' or 'call 911' — not a specific pharmacological intervention).
